Search results for "llvmlite emit_object link executable cross platform":

Title: Allow llvmlite to also link object code · Issue #311 - GitHub
URL: https://github.com/numba/llvmlite/issues/311
Content: ## Navigation Menu. # Search code, repositories, users, issues, pull requests... # Provide feedback. We read every piece of feedback, and take your input very seriously. # Saved searches. ## Use saved searches to filter your results more quickly. # Allow llvmlite to also link object code #311. ## Footer. it can also read this IR source code and generate a machine code object file using:. But it seems `llvmlite` does not have the functionality to actually link these object files into an executable. One has to do it by hand, e.g. using `gcc`:. `clang -o a.out file.o`. `"/usr/bin/ld" -z relro --hash-style=gnu --eh-frame-hdr -m elf_x86_64 -dynamic-linker /lib64/ld-linux-x86-64.so.2 -o c /usr/lib/gcc/x86_64-linux-gnu/6.1.1/../../../x86_64-linux-gnu/crt1.o /usr/lib/gcc/x86_64-linux-gnu/6.1.1/../../../x86_64-linux-gnu/crti.o /usr/lib/gcc/x86_64-linux-gnu/6.1.1/crtbegin.o -L/usr/lib/gcc/x86_64-linux-gnu/6.1.1 -L/usr/lib/gcc/x86_64-linux-gnu/6.1.1/../../../x86_64-linux-gnu -L/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu -L/lib/../lib64 -L/usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu -L/usr/lib/gcc/x86_64-linux-gnu/6.1.1/../../.. One does not need the object file for the JIT function (for numba) it seems. As it is now, one can generate object files, but the final step still requires either `gcc` or `clang` to be installed to do the linking. ### Footer navigation.

Title: How to cross platform link object file : r/Compilers
URL: https://www.reddit.com/r/Compilers/comments/106hzk5/how_to_cross_platform_link_object_file
Content: # How to cross platform link object file : r/Compilers. Skip to main contentHow to cross platform link object file : r/Compilers. # How to cross platform link object file. I'm trying to cross platform link (Link for any architecture from any architecture) object file (generated by LLVM). I generate an object file with LLVM for a specific platform, and I want to link it for the same platform. For Example, Using ARM64 macOS as host, and x86_64 Windows as target, an x86_64 Windows object file will be created by LLVM - but I don't know how to link it without worrying about the platform specific flags, runtime libraries, etc. I couldn't find any way to do that by using LLVM target-triple and LLVM::Linker. The object file is generated by standard LLVM target triple compilation to native object code. I know object files are platform specific, I generate an object file with LLVM for a specific platform, and I want to link it for the same platform.

Title: llvmlite.binding.TargetMachine.emit_object() creates incorrect ...

To see all available qualifiers, see our documentation. # llvmlite.binding.TargetMachine.emit\_object() creates incorrect executable type on Windows #181. ## Footer. I was messing around and trying to create an executable using llvmlite on my Windows machine, but I couldn't get it to work. I noticed that even though all of the default settings told me that the executable would be of the COFF format, it was an ELF executable. Here's an example shell run showing the issue:  . This generated file cannot be run by Windows. I'm running Windows 10, the beta that allows Bash on Windows, and Python 3.5 in a Conda Virtual Environment. I'm using the latest llvmlite version available on Conda. ### Type. ## Issue actions. ### Footer navigation.

Title: Cross Platform: Rust to LLVM IR and is LLVM IR is portable? - help - The Rust Programming Language Forum
URL: https://users.rust-lang.org/t/cross-platform-rust-to-llvm-ir-and-is-llvm-ir-is-portable/7767
Content: # Cross Platform: Rust to LLVM IR and is LLVM IR is portable? (Cross Platform): Is this LLVM IR portable to any platform that is supported by rust link (windows/Linux/Mac) and compile final executable ? LLVM's intermediate language is cross platform. Rust doesn't have to handle the specifics of compiling your code down to the target architecture/OS; it just has to turn it into IR, and then LLVM can take it from there. That said, you definitely don't have to have Rust emit IR and then manually compile it - cross compilation is really easy, especially if you use rustup to manage the different toolchains. For an explanation of what exactly MIR is, I'd recommend this blog post - Niko Matsakis has a golden gift for making complicated topics easy to understand, and I can guarantee I wouldn't do a better job trying to explain it here :stuck_out_tongue:. Yes, LLVM's intermediate language is cross platform.

Title: python - Link C in llvmlite
URL: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/36658726/link-c-in-llvmlite
Content: 1. Stack Internal Implement a knowledge platform layer to power your enterprise and AI tools. 3. Stack Ads Connect your brand to the world’s most trusted technologist communities. 4. Releases Keep up-to-date on features we add to Stack Overflow and Stack Internal. For this, i've already implemented some functions in C, and now i want to call these functions using `builder.call` from llvmlite. I have not found documentation or examples of how to do this. I could write the functions directly using llvmlite builders, but will be much quick, cleaner and easy do it in C. Do you have an example of how to perform the call? And if so, how do I then find the function to call it? On the LLVM IR side you can just declare the functions with the right signature (and no body), and insert calls to them like any other function. This is just like how in C you might call a function which is defined in another file.
